dr_pmt_v0.quote: a transit mode operating on roadways in response to requests from passengers or their agents to the transit operator, who groups rides together when possible and dispatches a vehicle to provide the rides. Vehicles do not operate over a fixed route or on a fixed schedule unless temporarily satisfying a special transit need. Many transit systems operate DR service to meet the requirements of the ADA.

verified.quote: The bunching effect can be measured in terms of headway adherence—the regularity of transit vehicle arrivals with respect to the scheduled headway. It is calculated as the coefficient of variation of headways cvh: the standard deviation of headways (representing the range of actual headways), divided by the average (mean) headway.
verified.citation: Transit Capacity and Quality of Service Manual, Third Edition (TCRP Report 165, Transportation Research Board), p. 5-30 (subscript formatting flattened by text extraction: "cvh" is c_v_h in print).

verified.quote: Headway adherence is calculated as the coefficient of variation of headway deviations (the standard deviation divided by the mean scheduled headway) […]
verified.citation: Transit Capacity and Quality of Service Manual, Third Edition (TCRP Report 165, Transportation Research Board), Calculation Example 3 ("Headway Deviation"), p. 5-92.

verified.quote: If the dataset included all of the trips for the study time period—for example, when all buses are AVL-equipped and their data are included in the dataset—then the population standard deviation would be used.
verified.citation: Transit Capacity and Quality of Service Manual, Third Edition (TCRP Report 165, Transportation Research Board), Calculation Example 3 ("Headway Deviation"), p. 5-92.

headway_owned.name: headway_adherence_v0
headway_owned.summary: Formula (the TCQSM Example-3 formulation, verified above; the pairing rule is Headway-owned):
headway_owned.formula:
scheduled_headway_i = s_{i+1} − s_i        (scheduled seconds; departure
                                            preferred, arrival fallback)
observed_headway_i  = t_{i+1} − t_i        (observed passage instants)
deviation_i         = observed_headway_i − scheduled_headway_i

cvh = pstdev({deviation_i}) / mean({scheduled_headway_i})

headway_owned.name: derive_stop_passages
headway_owned.summary: Owner: Headway. No external source defines this derivation. It turns `canonical.vehicle_positions` × the observed trips' scheduled stops (`canonical.stop_times` × `canonical.stops` × `canonical.trips`) into observed stop passages. Deterministic; versioned; changing any tolerance mints a new version. Implementation: `headway_calc/passages.py`.

verified.quote: this edition of the TCQSM defines 'on-time' as a departure from a timepoint as 1 min early to 5 min late or an arrival at the route terminal up to 5 min late.
verified.citation: Transit Capacity and Quality of Service Manual, Third Edition (TCRP Report 165, Transportation Research Board), Chapter 5 "Quality of Service Methods", p. 5-29 (margin note: "TCQSM definition of 'on-time.'").

verified.quote: on-time performance is the percent of schedule deviations (actual departure minus scheduled departure) that fall within a defined range (e.g., 1 min early to 5 min late)
verified.citation: Transit Capacity and Quality of Service Manual, Third Edition (TCRP Report 165, Transportation Research Board), p. 5-28 (the reliability-measures overview).

headway_owned.name: otp_v0
headway_owned.summary: Formula (window verified above; everything else Headway-owned):
headway_owned.formula:
deviation_i = observed_passage_i − scheduled_time_i          (seconds)
on_time_i   = (−E ≤ deviation_i ≤ L)                          E, L ≥ 0
OTP         = 100 × |{i : on_time_i}| / N                     N = usable passages

headway_owned.name: derive_stop_passages
headway_owned.summary: Owner: Headway. No external source defines this derivation. It turns `canonical.vehicle_positions` × the observed trips' scheduled stops (`canonical.stop_times` × `canonical.stops` × `canonical.trips`) into observed stop passages. Deterministic; versioned; changing any tolerance mints a new version. Implementation: `headway_calc/passages.py`.

pmt_v0.quote: Passenger Miles Traveled (PMT) is the sum of the distances each passenger traveled during the year.
pmt_v0.citation: Definition — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, p. 145

pmt_v0.quote: Transit agencies must report PMT only once under FB, because the other public or private vehicle is not moving under its own power while aboard the ferry service.
pmt_v0.citation: Definition — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, p. 145

pmt_v0.quote: Only Full Reporters report PMT data to the NTD.

pmt_v0.quote: a reasonable approach for estimating PMT.

pmt_v0.citation: 100% counts — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, p. 146

pmt_v0.citation: 100% counts — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, p. 146

pmt_v0.quote: The next benchmarking year is Report Year (RY) 2028
pmt_v0.citation: APC certification — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, pp. 146–149

pmt_v0.quote: the percent difference between manual and APC data in the sample, for both UPT and PMT, is less than 5 percent. The percent difference is calculated as (manual data − APC data) ∕ manual data
pmt_v0.citation: APC certification — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, pp. 146–149

pmt_v0.quote: less than 50 percent of the number of trips on APC-equipped vehicles
pmt_v0.citation: APC certification — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, pp. 146–149

pmt_v0.quote: Do not reject APC data from the sample due to discrepancies with the manual data.
pmt_v0.citation: APC certification — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, pp. 146–149

pmt_v0.quote: trips or blocks where the difference between boardings and alightings is greater than 10 percent, or trips where the passenger load drops below zero
pmt_v0.citation: APC scale-up method — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, pp. 151–152

pmt_v0.quote: If a group had no trips with valid data, use the average UPT and PMT from a similar group
pmt_v0.citation: APC scale-up method — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, pp. 151–152

pmt_v0.quote: Used all available APC data, which was less than 98 percent of trips, scaled up using a statistically valid method.
pmt_v0.citation: APC scale-up method — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, pp. 151–152

pmt_v0.quote: This year (FY 2026) is a mandatory sampling year. The next mandatory sampling year is FY 2029.
pmt_v0.citation: Sampling cycles — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, Exhibit 43, p. 153

pmt_v0.quote: estimate PMT data in a non-sampling year by multiplying the average trip length from the most recent mandatory year by the UPT for the current year,
pmt_v0.citation: Average-trip-length method — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, pp. 154–155, Exhibit 44
NTD Sampling Manual — 2026 NTD Full Reporting Policy Manual, p. 150

sampling_v0.quote: You must not determine the sample APTL as the average of the APTL across individual service units in the sample
sampling_v0.citation: Estimation with 100% UPT — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, §81
Template plans — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, Sections 50, 55–57
Table 41.01 — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, p. 4

sampling_v0.quote: One-way car trips, one-way train trips
sampling_v0.citation: Table 41.01 — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, p. 4

sampling_v0.quote: (a) New Mode – If you will be sampling and reporting for the first time this current report year for a particular mode that you do not already operate. … (b) New Type of Service – If you will be sampling and reporting this current report year for a particular type of service for the first time. … (c) No Sample Data – If you have reported your service to the NTD before through random sampling, but no longer have the original raw sample data.
sampling_v0.citation: §41.01 — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, p. 3

sampling_v0.quote: (a) You should not use it again if your next report year is your mandatory sampling year. After you have collected the sample data from this year, you should develop a template sampling plan with that sample data for your next report year. (b) You may use it again for the next report year if that year is not a mandatory sampling year.
sampling_v0.citation: §41.03 — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, p. 3

sampling_v0.quote: You should not use the ready-to-use sampling plans for commuter vanpool if your vanpool service does not serve commuters exclusively.
sampling_v0.citation: §41.05(a) — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, p. 3

sampling_v0.quote: (1) Base Option – you must estimate both UPT and PMT through random sampling. (2) APTL Option – you must report a 100% count of UPT, estimate the average passenger trip length (APTL) through random sampling, and obtain annual PMT by multiplying the 100% UPT with the estimated APTL. (3) Grouping Option – you must divide your bus routes into two groups by route length.
sampling_v0.citation: §41.07(c) — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, p. 4

sampling_v0.quote: (a) You may use the commonly used method based on a table of random numbers. (b) You may use any other method for random sampling as long as it meets these two criteria: (1) sampling under the method is random. (2) sampling under the method is without replacement. Without replacement means that the method will not select the same service unit more than once.
sampling_v0.citation: §63.03 — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, p. 19
§63.03 — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, p. 19

sampling_v0.quote: (a) You must determine the sample APTL for a given sample as the ratio of sample total PMT over sample total UPT for the following cases: (1) for the entire sample, (2) by type of service days, or (3) by service group. (b) You must not determine the sample APTL as the average of the APTL across individual service units in the sample.
sampling_v0.citation: §83.05 — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, p. 42

sampling_v0.quote: (a) You must use your 100% count of UPT as the expansion factor. (b) For estimating average daily PMT by type of service days, use your annual total 100% count of UPT by type of service days.
sampling_v0.citation: §83.01(a)/(b) — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, p. 42

sampling_v0.quote: If your sampling plan is not based on service grouping, you should multiply your sample APTL for the entire annual sample with your corresponding annual expansion factor (i.e., 100% count of annual UPT) to get an estimate of the annual total PMT.
sampling_v0.citation: §83.07(a) — FTA NTD Sampling Manual, March 31, 2009, p. 43

sscls_v0.quote: Assaults on a transit worker do not require an injury to be reportable on the S&S-50.

sscls_v0.quote: Meet an injury, fatality, substantial damage, or evacuation threshold; Include suicides, attempted suicides, and assaults or homicides that involve contact with a transit vehicle; Occur at a rail grade crossing or intersection; Involve an individual; Involve a rail transit vehicle and a second rail transit vehicle; or Include collisions that do not involve a transit vehicle but meet a threshold.
sscls_v0.citation: Collisions, rail — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 17

sscls_v0.quote: Meet an injury, fatality, property damage, or evacuation threshold; Involve a transit revenue vehicle and the towing away of any vehicles (transit or non-transit) from the scene; Include suicides, attempted suicides, assaults, or homicides resulting in an injury or fatality that involve contact with a transit vehicle; or Include collisions that do not involve a transit revenue vehicle but meet a threshold.
sscls_v0.citation: Collisions, non-rail/ferry — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 17

sscls_v0.quote: Evacuation of a transit facility or vehicle for life-safety reasons.
sscls_v0.citation: Evacuations — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 17

sscls_v0.quote: Evacuations to controlled rail right-of-way (excludes evacuation to a platform, except for life safety),
sscls_v0.citation: Evacuations — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 17

sscls_v0.quote: Both transit-directed evacuations and self-evacuations.
sscls_v0.citation: Evacuations — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 17

sscls_v0.quote: Both mainline and yard derailments and non-revenue vehicle derailments.
sscls_v0.citation: Derailments — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 17, rail only

sscls_v0.quote: movement of a rail transit vehicle on the mainline, yard, or shop that is uncommanded, uncontrolled, or unmanned due to an incapacitated, sleeping, or absent operator, or the failure of a rail transit vehicle's electrical, mechanical, or software system or subsystem.
sscls_v0.citation: Runaway Train — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 17, rail only, revenue vehicles

sscls_v0.quote: events that are NOT collisions, fires, security events, hazardous material spills, acts of God, or derailments … include slips, trips, falls, smoke events, fumes, runaway trains and electric shock. Only report these events when they meet either the fatality, evacuation, or property damage threshold or result in two or more injured persons. Other Safety Events that result in one person immediately transported from the scene for medical attention but do not trigger any other major reporting thresholds are reported on the Non-Major Summary Report.
sscls_v0.citation: Other Safety Events exception — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 22

sscls_v0.quote: Deaths of undetermined cause in a rail right-of-way that may be the result of collision or electrocution are reportable
sscls_v0.citation: Fatality nuances — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 20

sscls_v0.quote: if it is confirmed to have occurred within 30 days of the event.
sscls_v0.citation: Fatality nuances — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 20

sscls_v0.quote: Declarations or allegations of self-harm with no evident injury
sscls_v0.citation: Injury exclusions — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 22

sscls_v0.quote: reportable as a Rail Transit Collision (include one Other vehicle).
sscls_v0.citation: Example 4 solutions verbatim — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, pp. 18–19

sscls_v0.quote: reportable as an Other Safety Event

sscls_v0.quote: reportable to the NTD as a fatal rail collision.
sscls_v0.citation: Example 4 solutions verbatim — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, pp. 18–19

sscls_v0.quote: Reportable as a Major Safety Event due to the involvement of a transit vehicle.
sscls_v0.citation: Example 4 solutions verbatim — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, pp. 18–19

sscls_v0.quote: any harm to persons as a result of an event that requires immediate medical attention away from the scene. It does not include harm resulting from a drug overdose, exposure to the elements, illness, natural causes, or occupational safety events occurring in administrative buildings.
sscls_v0.citation: Injury — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 21

sscls_v0.quote: You must report each person transported away from the scene for medical attention as an injury, whether or not the person appears to be injured.
sscls_v0.citation: Injury — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 21

sscls_v0.quote: do not constitute immediate medical transportation away from the scene.
sscls_v0.citation: Injury — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 21

sscls_v0.quote: by transit vehicle, an ambulance, another emergency vehicle, private vehicle, or via stretcher to the hospital
sscls_v0.citation: Injury — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 21

sscls_v0.quote: Requires hospitalization for more than 48 hours within 7 days of the event; Results in a fracture of any bone (except simple fractures of fingers, toes, or nose); Causes severe hemorrhages, or nerve, muscle, or tendon damage; Involves an internal organ; or Involves second- or third-degree burns or any burns affecting more than 5 percent of the body surface.
sscls_v0.citation: Serious injury — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, rail, p. 21

sscls_v0.quote: estimated property damage equal to or exceeding $25,000, regardless of injuries or other thresholds. Estimated damage includes not only damage to transit property but also the cost of clearing wreckage and damage to all other vehicles and property involved in or affected by the event.
sscls_v0.citation: Property damage, non-rail — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 25

sscls_v0.quote: While the damage threshold for a major event is $25,000, any damage incurred must be included for any reportable event.
sscls_v0.citation: Property damage, non-rail — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 25

sscls_v0.quote: damage to any involved vehicles, facilities, equipment, rolling stock, or infrastructure that: Disrupts the operations of the rail transit agency, AND Adversely affects the structural strength, performance, or operating characteristics of the vehicle, facility, equipment, rolling stock, or infrastructure, such that it requires towing, rescue, on-site maintenance, or immediate removal prior to safe operation.
sscls_v0.citation: Substantial damage, rail — 2026 Safety & Security Policy Manual V1, p. 25

sscls_v0.quote: Cracked windows; Dents, bends, or small puncture holes in the body; Broken lights or mirrors; or Removal from service under the vehicle's own power for minor repair or maintenance, testing, or video and event recorder download.

upt_v0.quote: Unlinked Passenger Trips (UPT) are the number of boardings on public transportation vehicles during the fiscal year. Transit agencies must count passengers each time they board vehicles, no matter how many vehicles they use to travel from their origin to their destination. If a transit vehicle changes routes while passengers are onboard (interlining), transit agencies should not recount the passengers. Employees or contractors on transit agency business are not passengers.
upt_v0.citation: UPT definition — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 143

upt_v0.quote: Sometimes transit agencies performing 100 percent counts will miss passenger counts on some vehicle trips because of personnel problems or equipment failures. If these vehicle trips are 2 percent or less of the total, transit agencies should factor up the data to account for the missing trips. However, if the vehicle trips with missing data exceed 2 percent of total trips, agencies must have a qualified statistician approve the factoring method used to account for the missing percentage.
upt_v0.citation: 100% counts / missing-trip rule — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 146

upt_v0.quote: First, develop processes to throw out any trips with invalid APC data. … For example, agencies may flag trips or blocks where the difference between boardings and alightings is greater than 10 percent, or trips where the passenger load drops below zero.
upt_v0.citation: APC validation examples — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 151

upt_v0.quote: The use of APCs for NTD reporting requires FTA approval.
upt_v0.citation: APC certification — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, pp. 147–148

upt_v0.quote: FTA will only certify APC systems for NTD reporting if the percent difference between manual and APC data in the sample, for both UPT and PMT, is less than 5 percent.
upt_v0.citation: APC certification — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, pp. 147–148

upt_v0.quote: FTA will also only certify APC systems if the proportion of trips without valid APC data (the discard rate) is less than 50 percent of the number of trips on APC-equipped vehicles.
upt_v0.citation: APC certification — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, pp. 147–148

upt_v0.quote: The next benchmarking year is Report Year (RY) 2028.
upt_v0.citation: APC certification — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, pp. 147–148

upt_v0.quote: Minimum confidence of 95 percent; and Minimum precision level of ±10 percent.
upt_v0.citation: Sampling floor — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 149

voms_v0.quote: The MR-20 form requires agencies to report the following data points: • Unlinked Passenger Trips (UPT) • Actual Vehicle (Passenger Car) Revenue Hours • Actual Vehicle (Passenger Car) Revenue Miles • Vehicles Operated in Annual Maximum Service (VOMS)
voms_v0.citation: The four MR-20 data points, quoted — 2025 NTD Monthly and Weekly Reference Policy Manual, p. 32

voms_v0.quote: Full Reporters must report Monthly Ridership data for each mode of public transportation service that the agency operates … required to report on all modes reported on an agency's P-20 form.
voms_v0.citation: Per-mode reporting — 2025 NTD Monthly and Weekly Reference Policy Manual, p. 32

voms_v0.quote: may not meet FTA's confidence and precision levels for annual data (±10 percent precision for a 95 percent confidence level) but does meet FTA's requirements for reporting monthly data on the … MR-20.
voms_v0.citation: Monthly sampling relief — 2025 NTD Monthly and Weekly Reference Policy Manual, p. 32

voms_v0.quote: VOMS is the number of revenue vehicles/passenger cars operated to meet the maximum service requirement during the month … excludes atypical days or one-time special events
voms_v0.citation: Monthly VOMS — 2025 NTD Monthly and Weekly Reference Policy Manual, p. 33

vrh_v0.quote: A transit vehicle is in revenue service when it is providing public transportation and is available to carry passengers. Non-public transportation activities, such as exclusive school bus service and charter service are not considered revenue service. Revenue service includes both fare and fare-free services.
vrh_v0.citation: Revenue Service — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 128

vrh_v0.quote: Actual Vehicle Revenue Hours (VRH) and Actual Vehicle Revenue Miles (VRM) are the hours and miles vehicles travel while in revenue service. Revenue hours for conventional scheduled services include the following: • Running time • Layover/recovery time.
vrh_v0.citation: VRM/VRH — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 128

vrh_v0.quote: Revenue miles include the distances traveled during running time and layover/recovery time.
vrh_v0.citation: VRM/VRH — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 128

vrh_v0.quote: Usually, agencies schedule layover/recovery time at the end of each trip. … Layover time typically ranges from 10 to 20 percent of the running time.
vrh_v0.citation: Layover — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 128

vrh_v0.quote: VRM and VRH exclude the miles and hours related to the following: • Deadhead time • Operator training • Maintenance testing • Other non-revenue uses of the vehicles.
vrh_v0.citation: Exclusions — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 129

vrh_v0.quote: There are two different types of measures of VRH and VRM for rail service: train revenue hours/miles and passenger car revenue hours/miles. … a train with four passenger cars traveling one mile would be four passenger car revenue miles.
vrh_v0.citation: Rail measures — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 129

vrh_v0.quote: For DR service, revenue time includes all travel time from the point of the first passenger pick-up to the last passenger drop-off, as long as the vehicle does not return to the garage or dispatching point or have interruptions in service…
vrh_v0.citation: Demand Response — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 129

vrh_v0.quote: When transit vehicles are 'deadheading,' they operate closed door and do not carry passengers. Deadhead includes … Leaving or returning to the garage or yard facility …; Changing routes; When the driver does not have the duty to carry passengers.
vrh_v0.citation: Deadhead — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 129

vrh_v0.quote: Transit agencies must report accurate, true statistics for VRM (i.e., no estimates).
vrh_v0.citation: Accuracy requirement — 2026 NTD Policy Manual, Full Reporting, p. 135

vrh_v0.quote: Bus arrives at the end of the route, parks, and goes out of service… → Vehicle Revenue Hours: No
